g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Operating Systems - the essential software that manages computer hardware and resources.
An Operating System acts as an intermediary between the user and computer hardware, managing resources efficiently.
g.text: OS is system software that acts as an intermediary between computer
user and hardware. It manages resources and provides services for
application programs. The kernel is the one program running at all times.

g.data-narration: An OS has three primary goals: convenience for users, efficient resource utilization, and ability to evolve.
g.text: Three Goals of an Operating System

g.data-narration: The OS can be viewed as a Resource Manager that allocates hardware fairly, and as an Extended Machine providing abstractions.
g.text: Two Views of an Operating System

g.data-narration: A computer system has four layers: Users interact with Application Programs that run on the Operating System which controls Hardware.
g.text: Four Components of a Computer System

g.data-narration: In the 1940s-50s, computers had no OS. Programmers manually operated machines using plugboards with machine language.
g.text: Huge vacuum tube computers. Programming via plugboards in machine language.
One job at a time, completely manual. Programmer was also the operator.
Extremely slow setup, no user interaction,

g.data-narration: Batch systems (1955-1965) introduced punch cards and a resident monitor to run jobs sequentially in batches.
g.text: CPU idle during slow I/O operations.

g.data-narration: Multiprogramming keeps multiple jobs in memory. When one waits for I/O, CPU switches to another job for better utilization.
g.text: CPU switches when one waits for I/O.
Logical extension: rapid CPU switching between
users. Each gets small time slice (quantum).

g.data-narration: This diagram shows how multiprogramming works: three jobs occupy memory, and the CPU switches between them during I/O waits.
Compare batch systems, multiprogramming, and time-sharing: interactivity, response time, CPU usage, and user count differ significantly.
Modern OS types include Personal Computer OS, Distributed Systems, Mobile OS, Real-Time OS, and Cloud OS.
Operating systems are categorized by their purpose: Batch for sequential jobs, Time-sharing for interactivity, Real-Time for critical timing.
The OS serves dual roles: managing hardware resources efficiently while providing abstractions that simplify application development.
For exams, memorize Silberschatz's definition, the three OS goals, and the layered component diagram - these are frequently asked.
Thank you for learning about Operating Systems. Remember: OS is the bridge between you and computer hardware, making computing accessible and efficient.
g.text: OS bridges users and hardware - making computers convenient, efficient, and evolvable.
